_source: Federal Reserve Survey of Consumer Finances (SCF) 2022 and FDIC National Survey of Unbanked and Underbanked Households 2021. Values in USD.
_notes: Distributions represent typical ranges for legitimate accounts. Fraudulent mule accounts tend to show sudden balance spikes inconsistent with the account holder's profile — a signal for anomaly detection.

checking_standard.description: Standard personal checking account — majority of US households
checking_standard.notes: Highly right-skewed distribution — median much lower than mean. Most accounts maintain low balances with occasional payroll spikes.

checking_premium.description: Premium or relationship checking — higher income households, minimum balance requirements
checking_premium.notes: Wider variance. These accounts are targets for account takeover because large transactions are less anomalous.

savings_standard.description: Standard savings account — FDIC-insured, lower APY
savings_standard.notes: Used for emergency funds; typical balance accumulates slowly from recurring transfers.

savings_high_yield.description: High-yield savings — online banks (Marcus, Ally, SoFi), earns 4–5% APY
savings_high_yield.notes: Attracts higher-income savers. Unusual for frequent withdrawals — deviations are high-signal anomalies.

student_checking.description: Student checking — typically 18–24 age bracket, low or no minimum balance
student_checking.notes: Very low balances. Student accounts receiving large ACH credits are immediately anomalous. Commonly recruited as mule accounts due to financial vulnerability.

business_checking_small.description: Small business checking — sole proprietors, LLCs under $1M annual revenue
business_checking_small.notes: Higher transaction velocity than personal accounts — legitimate cover for frequent large transfers. Business accounts are harder to flag on velocity alone.

business_checking_medium.description: Medium business checking — companies $1M–$50M revenue, payroll accounts
business_checking_medium.notes: Very high transaction volume and amounts. Wire transfers in the $50,000–$250,000 range are normal. Least anomalous for large pass-through transfers — highest mule value if compromised.

money_market_account.description: Money market account — higher interest, limited monthly withdrawals
money_market_account.notes: Regulation D limits 6 withdrawals/month; excess triggers fees. Unusual withdrawal velocity is flagged automatically by most core banking systems.

senior_checking.description: Checking accounts for adults 65+ — often fixed-income recipients
senior_checking.notes: Regular recurring income (Social Security, pension). Deviations from monthly patterns are high-signal. Frequently targeted in romance scams and authorized push payment fraud.

recently_opened_personal.description: Personal checking account opened within the last 90 days
recently_opened_personal.notes: Very low baseline balance. New accounts receiving large transfers within days of opening are one of the strongest AML red flags. Velocity rules typically apply stricter limits for the first 90 days.

synthetic_identity_account.description: Account opened using a synthetic identity (fabricated SSN + real address/DOB combination)
synthetic_identity_account.notes: Synthetic ID accounts often show a 'build-up' pattern: small activity for 6–18 months to establish credit history, then rapid extraction. Account age and credit bureau thin-file status are key signals.
